messages.content:
YOUR ROLE: You are the partner running a pre-mortem on this market model for a
paying client. The client's own team has already read every number in the
ledger, including every sensitivity band. Your reputation rests on surfacing
what THEY would not list: risks that look benign on the surface but could kill
the venture, argued mechanistically, anchored in the model.

HARD REQUIREMENTS — a hypothesis that misses any of these will be rejected by
a validator before any human reads it:
1. targetNodes: real ledger node ids; perturbation ops only on FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes.
2. mechanism: at least 3 steps (trigger → propagation → model impact), EVERY
   step carrying refs — "ledger:<nodeId>", "digest:<group>#c<n>", or
   "profile:<field>". A step you cannot reference is a step you are guessing.
3. falsifier: the single concrete observation that would kill this risk. If you
   cannot name one, it is not a claim.
4. whyMissable: why a competent founder's own risk list omits this. "It's
   non-obvious" is not an argument; name the surface appearance that hides it.
5. likelihood: a probability with a basis ("evidence" | "base-rate" |
   "judgment") and a rationale. No naked numbers.
6. indicators: at least 2 observable early warnings, each with WHERE to watch
   (a named tender portal, an operator's capex call, a standards docket, a
   vendor's price list) and the reading that should trigger re-assessment.
7. mitigations: at least 2. Where the right move is to buy information, use
   type "information" and name the ledger node it de-risks (voiNodeId) — its
   value will be computed, not asserted.

QUOTAS (per lens): at least one hypothesis self-tagged expectedObviousness
"low", and at least one whose perturbation targets NONE of tamBase /
serviceableFactor / obtainableFactor — the tornado already covers those bands;
look past them.

MINE THE RESIDUALS: the highest-yield material is where sources disagree with
the curated value (the digests' "tensions"), where searches came back empty
(the digests' "absences" — an unquantified market is a risk, not a comfort),
where a derivation's crossCheck diverges from its primary method, and where a
precise-looking value carries maturity "needs-source".

HOW YOUR CLAIM BECOMES A NUMBER (the perturbation contract):
- You never state € impacts. You emit machine-readable ops; the engine computes
  ΔTAM/ΔSAM/ΔYAM by re-running the real funnel. Any € figure you write in prose
  will be stripped.
- Ops: {nodeId, op: "set"|"scale"|"add", value} rewrite one leaf's value;
  {nodeId, op: "exclude"} removes a dimension cell from the venture's reachable
  scope ("unavailable to us" — foreclosure), which is different from shrinking it.
- Ops apply in array order. Ratio nodes must stay inside [0,1]; tamBase must stay > 0.
- Only FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes may be perturbed (marked in the ledger you receive).
  shape.cagr and shape.cr3 are context — a demand-pace or concentration risk must
  be translated into the funnel nodes it would actually move (tamBase level,
  serviceableFactor reach, obtainableFactor pace, or a share).
- Sibling shares are NOT re-normalized when you move one share (documented v1
  simplification). If you mean a mix-SHIFT, perturb multiple siblings; if you
  mean "this slice is smaller in absolute terms", one op is correct.
- Perturbing an assumption leaf overrides whatever the user set: your claim is
  about the world, not about their slider.

BANNED GENRES (emitting one of these wastes your slot):
- "Find 2 customers to talk to and get an LOI" — an action item, not a risk. It names no node, no mechanism, no falsifier.
- "Do research on customer buying behaviour" — a homework assignment any consultant could write for any company.
- "Competition is strong / the market is concentrated" — the ledger already states CR3. Restating a fact is not a risk; the risk is a MECHANISM that concentration enables.
- "The market may be smaller than expected" — the tamBase band (240–520) already says this. Only worth saying if you name WHICH derivation hop fails and WHY.
- "Regulatory changes could impact the business" — name the instrument, the compliance artifact it requires, and the date it lands, or drop it.
- "The band's low end might be true" with no new reason — the band is already in the model; you are paid for what is NOT in the model.

GOLD EXEMPLAR (the target register — do NOT repeat its content):
{
 "id": "busway-hollowing-hyperscale",
 "title": "Busway + OCP power shelves hollow out the hyperscale 44% from inside the scope boundary",
 "narrative": "The model excludes busbars and power shelves as out-of-scope substitutes — a scoping footnote. But hyperscale is 44% of TAM, and it is precisely the segment whose new AI-density builds are moving power distribution from rack PDUs to overhead busway feeding OCP-style in-rack power shelves. If that architecture becomes the default for new CE hyperscale capacity, the largest slice of this TAM is not 'ours to win slowly' — it is structurally shrinking while the headline number stays flat. The exclusion list quietly became the market thesis.",
 "category": "boundary",
 "targetNodes": [
  "seg.hyperscale"
 ],
 "mechanism": [
  {
   "step": "trigger",
   "text": "New hyperscale AI-density builds adopt busway + in-rack DC power shelves instead of discrete rack PDUs",
   "refs": [
    "digest:seg-mix#c?",
    "ledger:seg.hyperscale"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "propagation",
   "text": "CE hyperscale capacity additions (the growth the 9% CAGR is made of) carry falling rack-PDU content per MW; replacement demand in legacy halls does not offset it",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:shape.cagr",
    "digest:tam-base#c?"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "model-impact",
   "text": "The hyperscale share of the rack-PDU market shrinks in absolute terms — seg.hyperscale 0.44 overstates the addressable slice for a new entrant's window",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:seg.hyperscale"
   ]
  }
 ],
 "whyMissable": "Substitutes were explicitly considered and excluded — the boundary decision looks handled. The kill path is not 'we misjudged the boundary' but 'the boundary moved into the biggest segment while the TAM base year stayed put'.",
 "expectedObviousness": "low",
 "falsifier": "2025–26 CE hyperscale fit-out specs (or Schneider/Vertiv rack-power order mixes) showing intelligent rack PDUs holding share in new AI-density halls.",
 "likelihood": {
  "value": 0.35,
  "rationale": "OCP power-shelf adoption is documented for AI racks, but CE colocation and enterprise retrofits (56% of TAM) keep buying rack PDUs; timing within a 12-month window is uncertain.",
  "basis": "evidence"
 },
 "perturbation": [
  {
   "nodeId": "seg.hyperscale",
   "op": "set",
   "value": 0.22,
   "note": "≈half of the hyperscale slice migrates to busway/shelf architectures"
  }
 ],
 "indicators": [
  {
   "signal": "Rack-power architecture specified in new CE hyperscale tenders (busway/shelf vs PDU)",
   "where": "Frankfurt/Amsterdam data-center fit-out tenders; OCP regional summit deployment talks",
   "threshold": "≥2 flagship CE builds specifying shelf-based distribution",
   "updates": "increases"
  },
  {
   "signal": "Incumbent rack-PDU revenue mix commentary (Vertiv/Schneider earnings)",
   "where": "Quarterly earnings calls, rack-power product-line commentary",
   "threshold": "PDU line flat while busway line grows double-digit",
   "updates": "increases"
  }
 ],
 "mitigations": [
  {
   "action": "Commission a CE rack-power architecture survey across the 2026 hyperscale build pipeline",
   "type": "information",
   "voiNodeId": "seg.hyperscale"
  },
  {
   "action": "Qualify the product line for colocation/enterprise retrofit first, where PDU form-factor is sticky",
   "type": "strategic"
  }
 ]
}

content: {"hypotheses":[{"id":"europe-share-arithmetic-ghost","title":"The 'Europe ≈ 30%' hop is an arithmetic ghost — no source states it, and regional datapoints put Europe closer to 24%","narrative":"The entire €320M chain multiplies GVR's $2.81B global figure by 'Europe ≈ 30%', a number no publisher states — it is inferred by eyeballing the residual after North America's 38%. But the only regional revenue datapoints in the corpus cut the other way: the US alone is $579.7M of a $2.1B global intelligent-PDU market (~28% for one country), APAC is uniformly described as the fastest-growing region, and Germany — half of the CE weight — is forecast to grow its intelligent-PDU segment at only 5.2–5.3%, roughly half the global rate. If PDU revenue is more NA/APAC-skewed than DC capacity, Europe sits at 22–25%, not 30%, and every downstream node inherits the error. This is not 'the band's low end' — it is a specific hop whose value was never observed, drifting in a specific direction.","category":"fact","targetNodes":["tamBase"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"The 30% Europe multiplier in tamBase's derivation is unsourced: GVR states only NA = 38%, and no source found anywhere states a Europe rack-PDU revenue share","refs":["ledger:tamBase","digest:tam-global-crosscheck#c1"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"Regional evidence skews the residual away from Europe: US intelligent-PDU market alone is $579.7M of $2.1B global (~28%), China is on a 6.4% CAGR toward $498M, and Germany's intelligent-PDU CAGR (5.2–5.3%) runs at roughly half the 9.7% global rack-PDU rate — meaning Europe's share is both lower than 30% and declining through the venture window","refs":["digest:tam-global-crosscheck#c15","digest:tam-global-crosscheck#c13","digest:tam-global-crosscheck#c14","digest:tam-global-crosscheck#c0"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"Europe at ~24% instead of 30% rescales the whole scope-down chain multiplicatively — the curated €320M point value sits above the recomputed primary derivation, and SAM/YAM shrink proportionally","refs":["ledger:tamBase"]}],"whyMissable":"The hop looks sourced because it sits inside a derivation expression quoting Grand View by name — the citation is real, but it covers only the NA share. A team auditing the ledger sees '[est.; NA = 38% per same source]' and reads it as a bracketed footnote, not as the single unobserved multiplier the entire funnel rests on.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"Any primary source stating Europe's rack-PDU revenue share at ≥28% — e.g., the Omdia Rack PDU Annual Tracker regional table or the IndexBox EU Rack PDUs headline reconciled against GVR's global base.","likelihood":{"value":0.4,"rationale":"Every regional datapoint in the corpus (US intelligent-PDU absolute size, Germany's sub-global CAGR, APAC growth framing) points below 30%, but none directly measures the Europe rack-PDU share, so the direction is evidenced while the magnitude is not.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"tamBase","op":"scale","value":0.8,"note":"Europe share 30% → ~24% of the global rack-PDU base"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Regional revenue split in the next Omdia Rack PDU Annual Market Analysis or GVR report edition","where":"Omdia rack-PDU tracker (2025 update) regional tables; Grand View Research report refresh","threshold":"Europe stated at <27% of global rack-PDU revenue","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"EMEA vs Americas revenue mix for rack-power product lines in incumbent segment reporting","where":"Vertiv 10-K segment disclosures; Legrand universe-by-region commentary in annual results","threshold":"EMEA rack-power growth persistently below Americas by >4pp","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Commission the IndexBox EU Rack PDUs report ($4,000) to replace the inferred Europe share with a directly-scoped EU headline figure","type":"information","voiNodeId":"tamBase","note":"Named in the ledger as the promotion path; collapses the unsourced 30% hop entirely"},{"action":"Rebuild tamBase bottom-up from CE energized MW × PDUs-per-rack × ASP, bypassing the global-share hop altogether","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":"A capacity-intensity build needs no regional revenue share and cross-checks the top-down chain"}]},{"id":"intelligent-subset-inflation","title":"The TAM counts basic and metered PDUs the venture doesn't sell — and Germany's intelligent slice is growing at half the assumed rate","narrative":"The venture sells intelligent rack PDUs; tamBase is scoped as the rack-PDU market, all classes — basic, metered, switched, intelligent. The definitional gap is treated as harmless because 'intelligent is the growth segment'. But the corpus says the intelligent subset is materially smaller than the headline ($2.1B of $2.81B globally at best, with publisher definitions in open contradiction), and — critically — Germany, which carries 50% of the geographic weight, shows intelligent-PDU growth of only 5.2–5.3% CAGR, versus the 9% assumed for the market. Add 60–70% import dependence in Eastern Europe served at low price points, and the CE-relevant intelligent base could be 60–70% of the headline €320M. The venture is sizing the market for a product category it only partially competes in.","category":"boundary","targetNodes":["tamBase"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"Product scope (intelligent PDUs) is narrower than the tamBase scope (all rack PDUs): global intelligent-PDU market is sized at $2.1B (2024) against the $2.81B all-class rack-PDU base — roughly a 25–30% haircut before any regional adjustment","refs":["profile:Product","digest:tam-global-crosscheck#c11","digest:tam-global-crosscheck#c0"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"CE demand skews toward the non-intelligent end: Germany's intelligent-PDU segment grows at only 5.2–5.3% CAGR (half the market rate), and Eastern Europe runs 60–70% import dependence consistent with price-led basic/metered purchasing — so the intelligent share of CE spend is lower than the global mix, not higher","refs":["digest:tam-global-crosscheck#c13","digest:tam-global-crosscheck#c14","digest:tam-base#c15"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"The venture-relevant base is the CE intelligent slice, plausibly ~70% of the €320M headline — every downstream funnel stage (SAM reach, Year-1 obtainable) is computed against demand the product cannot address","refs":["ledger:tamBase","ledger:serviceableFactor"]}],"whyMissable":"'All segments' was an explicit, recorded scoping answer, so the boundary looks deliberately chosen rather than accidentally loose. And because intelligent PDUs are universally described as the fastest-growing segment (IndexBox: 10–12% annually), the definitional gap reads as upside — nobody checks whether the fast-growing slice is also the small slice in the specific geography carrying half the weight.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"A sourced EU/CE product-class split (Omdia tracker sub-segmentation or IndexBox EU country tables) showing intelligent/switched PDUs at ≥85% of CE rack-PDU revenue.","likelihood":{"value":0.35,"rationale":"The intelligent-vs-all-class gap is documented globally, and the Germany sub-global CAGR is a direct datapoint; but publisher definitions of 'intelligent PDU' contradict each other (MRF sizes it larger than the whole rack-PDU market), so the CE haircut magnitude is genuinely uncertain.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"tamBase","op":"scale","value":0.7,"note":"Restrict the base to the CE intelligent-PDU slice (~70% of the all-class headline)"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"SKU mix (basic/metered vs switched/intelligent) in CE distributor catalogs and price lists","where":"Rittal, Bachmann, and CE master-distributor (e.g., Also/Ingram DC-infrastructure) published price lists","threshold":"Basic+metered SKUs >40% of listed rack-PDU line items in DE/PL catalogs","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"PDU class specified in German and Polish colocation fit-out tenders","where":"Frankfurt colo tender documents; Polish public-sector DC procurement portal (platformazakupowa)","threshold":"≥half of tenders specifying metered-only or basic PDUs","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Obtain the Omdia Rack PDU tracker with product-class sub-segmentation to pin the intelligent share of EU revenue","type":"information","voiNodeId":"tamBase","note":"Directly measures the venture-relevant slice instead of the all-class headline"},{"action":"Add a metered mid-tier SKU to the launch line so the addressable base spans the metered+intelligent classes rather than intelligent-only","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Widens product scope to match the sized market instead of shrinking the market to match the product"}]},{"id":"statista-pipeline-mw-inflation","title":"The CE-7 = 39.8% hop is built on Statista MW that don't reconcile with anyone else's Europe — pipeline capacity counted as PDU-buying capacity","narrative":"Every geo ratio and the CE-7-of-Europe multiplier come from one Statista table whose summed 2024 country figures (~7.6GW) exceed CBRE's 4,726MW European colocation supply and even CBRE's 5.8GW total European DC capacity. Something in Statista's 'colocation and scale-colocation IT power' definition counts capacity that is contracted or under construction, not energized — Germany 'growing' 24.8% in a single forecast year while CBRE reports only 937MW of new supply for all of Europe is the tell. PDUs are bought at fit-out, per energized MW. If the padding is concentrated in the growth markets (Germany, Poland) rather than the mature FLAPD stock, the CE-7 share of Europe is overstated on a delivered-capacity basis — and the venture's Year-1 window is sized against halls that won't be racked for two years.","category":"fact","targetNodes":["tamBase"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"Statista's summed 2024 per-country colo figures (~7.6GW) exceed CBRE's Q4-2024 European colocation supply (4,726MW) and approach-exceed CBRE's 5.8GW total European DC capacity — an unreconciled >1.6x scope gap in the sole source behind the CE-7 apportionment","refs":["digest:geo-region-denominator#c1","digest:geo-region-denominator#c3","digest:geo-region-denominator#c5"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"The inflation is growth-market-skewed: Statista shows Germany jumping 1,737→2,167 MW (+24.8%) and Poland +27.6% in 2026, while CBRE counts only 937MW of new supply delivered across all of Europe in 2025 — meaning the CE-7 numerator carries proportionally more announced/contracted capacity than the FLAPD-heavy denominator","refs":["digest:geo-region-denominator#c2","digest:geo-region-denominator#c7","ledger:geo.DE"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"The 39.8% CE-7-of-Europe multiplier in tamBase's derivation is overstated on an energized-MW basis; rack-PDU spend follows fit-out of live halls, so the CE base for the venture's 12-month window is smaller than the ledger's €320M implies","refs":["ledger:tamBase"]}],"whyMissable":"The geo nodes look robust because they are internally cross-checked (BMWK, DDCA, Mordor, CBRE Suisse) — but every cross-check validates a different country against a different scope, and none reconciles the Statista Europe-wide sum against an independent Europe total. Internal consistency of ratios masks a scope error in the level.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"A reconciliation showing Statista's country figures are live energized IT power — e.g., CBRE country pages independently confirming Germany at ~1,700MW+ live colocation in 2025.","likelihood":{"value":0.3,"rationale":"The 7.6GW-vs-4.7/5.8GW gap is documented and large, but part of it may be coverage (30 countries vs CBRE's top-15) rather than pipeline padding; the direction of CE-skew is inferred, not measured.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"tamBase","op":"scale","value":0.85,"note":"CE-7 share of Europe ~39.8% → ~34% on a delivered/energized-capacity basis"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Delivered (not announced) MW per CE market versus Statista's year-over-year increments","where":"CBRE quarterly 'Europe Data Centres Figures' (Frankfurt/secondary-market delivery tables)","threshold":"Frankfurt+Berlin deliveries <200MW in 2026 against Statista's implied +430MW for Germany","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Energization delays cited by CE operators (grid connection queues in Frankfurt, Amsterdam moratorium after-effects)","where":"Operator capex/earnings calls (Vantage, NTT GDC, Data4) and German TSO (TenneT/Amprion) connection-queue disclosures","threshold":"Multiple operators pushing CE energization dates by ≥12 months","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Rebuild the CE-7 apportionment on a delivered-capacity series (CBRE quarterly figures) instead of the Statista snapshot, and diff the two","type":"information","voiNodeId":"tamBase","note":"One analyst-week; directly tests whether the 39.8% hop survives a scope change"},{"action":"Time the sales-capacity ramp to tracked fit-out starts rather than announced MW — hire against delivered capacity milestones","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Converts the sizing risk into a pacing decision instead of a fixed-cost bet"}]},{"id":"colo-scope-geography-misallocation","title":"Apportioning all-segment TAM by colocation MW plants 29% of the market in the wrong countries — and the Year-1 channel plan inherits the error","narrative":"The geo split apportions the entire TAM — including the 29% enterprise on-premise segment — by colocation MW. But enterprise PDU demand follows installed IT load, not colo halls, and the two geographies diverge sharply in CE: Germany's installed load (BMWK >2,700MW) dwarfs its colo figure (1,737MW), Switzerland is famously self-operation-heavy (850MW installed vs 274MW colo), while the Netherlands is the mirror image — an Amsterdam colo hub with a thin enterprise base. The ledger's own cross-checks show exactly this (DE 0.50 colo vs 0.42 installed; CH 0.08 vs 0.13) and then average past it. For a venture whose first-year channel is 'large operators' concentrated in Frankfurt and Amsterdam, the model quietly relocates a third of the TAM into the two metros it happens to be targeting — flattering the reachable share while the real enterprise demand sits in Swiss banks, Austrian utilities, and Polish industrials the channel never touches.","category":"model-structure","targetNodes":["geo.DE","geo.NL","geo.CH","geo.PL"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"The geo dimension uses a single colo-MW apportionment for all segments, but 29% of the TAM is enterprise on-premise whose geography follows installed load — and EUDCA confirms roughly one-third of Europe's IT power is outside commercial colo/hyperscale","refs":["ledger:seg.enterprise","digest:geo-region-denominator#c9","ledger:geo.DE"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"Where colo and installed scopes diverge, the ledger's own cross-checks disagree systematically: DE 0.50 (colo) vs 0.42 (BMWK installed), CH 0.08 vs 0.13 (self-operated-inclusive), PL 0.07 vs 0.10 (Mordor installed) — the colo lens over-weights the Amsterdam/Frankfurt hub countries and under-weights self-operation-heavy markets","refs":["ledger:geo.DE","ledger:geo.CH","ledger:geo.PL","ledger:geo.NL"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"Blending the enterprise 29% on an installed-load geography shifts the mix out of NL and DE toward CH and PL; the venture's operator-first channel (Frankfurt/Amsterdam colo) then covers a smaller true share of the TAM than the current split implies, compressing the reachable slice behind serviceableFactor","refs":["profile:Who is the primary buyer you'll sell through first?","ledger:serviceableFactor","ledger:cust.operator-large"]}],"whyMissable":"Every geo node individually documents its colo-vs-installed cross-check and brackets it in the band — the divergence looks handled. What no band captures is the correlation: all four scope errors point the same way at once (toward the colo hubs), and they interact with a segment split and a channel choice that live on different ledger dimensions. Node-by-node review cannot see a cross-dimensional misallocation.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"A vendor country-revenue split (Omdia country tables or a Schneider/Vertiv CE distributor sell-through report) showing rack-PDU sales proportional to colocation MW rather than installed IT load.","likelihood":{"value":0.4,"rationale":"The scope divergences are documented in the ledger's own cross-checks for four of seven countries and all point the same direction; the open question is only how tightly enterprise PDU refresh demand tracks installed load, which is unmeasured.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"geo.DE","op":"set","value":0.46,"note":"Blend colo-weighted 71% of TAM with installed-load-weighted 29% enterprise slice"},{"nodeId":"geo.NL","op":"set","value":0.24,"note":"NL enterprise base thin relative to its Amsterdam colo weight"},{"nodeId":"geo.CH","op":"set","value":0.11,"note":"Self-operation-heavy Switzerland gains weight under installed-load blending"},{"nodeId":"geo.PL","op":"set","value":0.09,"note":"Poland installed load (660MW Mordor) vs colo 228MW pulls share up"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Share of CE rack-PDU demand originating from enterprise/self-operated sites vs colo fit-outs","where":"BMWK annual 'German data centre landscape' update (installed vs colo split); CBRE Suisse commercial-vs-self-operated capacity report","threshold":"Enterprise/self-operated share of national capacity holding >35% in DE/CH","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Where early inbound demand actually comes from during launch","where":"Venture's own CRM geography vs the model's geo weights; distributor sell-through reports from CE master distributors","threshold":"≥30% of qualified pipeline from outside DE+NL within 6 months","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Split the geo apportionment by segment: colo MW for hyperscale/colo slices, installed IT load for the enterprise slice, and recompute the funnel","type":"information","voiNodeId":"geo.DE","note":"Pure re-derivation from data already in the ledger's cross-checks — near-zero cost"},{"action":"Add a Swiss/Austrian enterprise reseller to the Year-1 channel plan rather than relying solely on Frankfurt/Amsterdam operator relationships","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Hedges the channel against the geography the colo lens hides"}]},{"id":"crosscheck-ceiling-contamination","title":"The €495M cross-check ceiling grows at 21.23% CAGR — it is measuring the substitute architecture, and the band's upside is a mirage","narrative":"The high end of tamBase's band (€520M, worth +€3.3M YAM at the band edge) rests on one calculation: BIS's €2.45B Europe 'PDUs + PSUs' figure × an unsourced ~55% PDU share. But that BIS category grows at 21.23% CAGR — a rate no mature rack-PDU market exhibits (every rack-PDU-only source says 5.4–9.8%). A 21% growth rate in a 'PDUs and PSUs' category is the signature of OCP power shelves and rack-level DC power supplies — precisely the substitute architecture the model's boundary excludes. The ceiling isn't a generous estimate of the venture's market; it's a measurement of the thing eating the venture's market. If the board sizes hiring, inventory, or the raise against the band midpoint on the theory that upside and downside are symmetric, they are capitalizing a number that contains the competitor architecture's growth.","category":"model-structure","targetNodes":["tamBase"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"The BIS/R&M Europe 'PDUs + PSUs' base ($2.45B → $20.05B, 21.23% CAGR) used for the crossCheck ceiling grows 2–4x faster than every rack-PDU-only estimate (5.4–9.8% across five publishers) — a growth signature inconsistent with the rack-PDU scope it is being used to validate","refs":["digest:tam-base#c2","digest:tam-base#c3","digest:tam-global-crosscheck#c7","digest:tam-global-crosscheck#c6"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"The excess growth must sit in the PSU/DC-power-shelf portion of the category — the excluded substitute — and the '~55% PDU share' applied to strip it out is unsourced, so the €495M ceiling inherits an unknown fraction of substitute-architecture revenue; MarketsAndMarkets' 19.5% CAGR for its broader rack+floor PDU scope shows the same contamination pattern","refs":["ledger:tamBase","digest:tam-base#c4","digest:tam-base#c5"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"tamBase's true uncertainty is asymmetric: the primary chain supports €240–320, while the 520 high edge is scope-contaminated — any planning premised on the band's symmetric upside (the +€200M dTam edge in the affordances) overstates the venture's realistic ceiling","refs":["ledger:tamBase"]}],"whyMissable":"Cross-checks exist to add confidence, and this one is dutifully labeled 'broader scope used as the cross-check ceiling' — the caveat is in the ledger. What the caveat hides is that the broader scope isn't just broader, it's adversarial: its growth is the substitute's growth. A wide band reads as honest uncertainty; nobody asks whether one edge of the band is made of a different market.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"BIS/R&M report segmentation showing the PDU-only sub-segment at ≥55% of the category AND growing ≤10% CAGR — which would confirm the ceiling as a legitimate rack-PDU reading.","likelihood":{"value":0.45,"rationale":"The CAGR incompatibility is arithmetic and visible in the cited excerpts (21.23% vs 5.4–9.8% for the same nominal product), and the 55% PDU share is admitted as unsourced; the only uncertainty is how much of the excess growth is PSU/shelf revenue vs report optimism.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"tamBase","op":"set","value":280,"note":"Recentre on the primary scope-down chain after discarding the contaminated €495M ceiling; effective distribution 240–320"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Sub-segment split (PDU vs PSU/power shelf) and per-segment CAGRs in the BIS Europe report sample pages or ToC","where":"Research and Markets / giiresearch listing pages for the Europe PDUs+PSUs report;
